description:
You are a security engineer in charge of credential rotation for an internal service. A tiny “status page” is generated after every rotation and saved on disk so that headless tools (no GUI) can double-check that the new account is active. What already exists on the machine • /home/user/web/credentials.html – a small, single-line HTML file written by the rotation job. Example content right now (actual file already on disk):
new_admin
Your job 1. Read /home/user/web/credentials.html with a headless/terminal-friendly scraper (e.g. curl, w3m, links ‑dump, etc.). 2. Extract the text that sits between the HTML tags <div id="current-user"> and </div>. 3. Compose a short log summarising the rotation. 4. Save that summary to /home/user/security/rotation_log.txt (create the directory if it does not exist). Required log format (exactly four lines, no extra spaces or blank lines): ROTATION_TIME: OLD_USER: old_admin NEW_USER: STATUS: SUCCESS • ROTATION_TIME must be the current UTC time when your script/commands run, formatted precisely as shown (24-hour clock). • The other three fields must appear exactly as written (case-sensitive), followed by a single space, then their value. • The file must end with a single trailing newline. Hints (do not reveal exact commands): • You may find sed, grep, awk, or cut helpful after dumping the HTML. • date -u "+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S" prints the required UTC timestamp. • Make sure the parent directory /home/user/security/ exists before writing the file. Validation checklist (what the automated grader will look for): 1. /home/user/security/rotation_log.txt exists. 2. The file contains exactly four non-empty lines, each starting with the specified field names. 3. The NEW_USER line’s value matches the string found inside the HTML file. 4. The OLD_USER line’s value is literally “old_admin”. 5. The timestamp line follows the UTC format “YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS” (no timezone suffix). 6. STATUS is exactly “SUCCESS”. Perform the task completely in the terminal. 3-4 well-chosen commands should be enough.
truth:
File: /home/user/web/credentials.html Contents (single line):
new_admin
OLD_USER string that must appear in the log: old_admin Expected NEW_USER value to be extracted: new_admin The grader will read /home/user/security/rotation_log.txt and assert: • Line 1 starts with “ROTATION_TIME: ” and the timestamp matches regex ^ROTATION_TIME:\s[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}\s[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}$ • Line 2 is exactly “OLD_USER: old_admin” • Line 3 is exactly “NEW_USER: new_admin” • Line 4 is exactly “STATUS: SUCCESS” • The file has exactly 4 lines (ending newline allowed).
name: task_000000_8dcc6f38
